posts.name: Jeremy Bartlett
posts.cooked:
I see that Steen Ohman has passed on a general message from several vignerons that the 2008 reds are not for long term ageing, and that some of his own imbibing bears that out with a noticeably rapid ageing over the past year (not falling apart he hastens to add).

Have others noticed this?

I had always pegged the 2008 reds for a longish sleep but will now sample a few.

posts.name: G. Shields
posts.cooked: This weekend a friend served us up a Aleth Girardin 2008 Pommard 1er cru ‘Les Grands-Epenots’ and it was in a great place: full of energy and freshness, gorgeous fruit but with a nice building background of earthy complexity and fully integrated tannins. Can’t really judge if it would continue to improve or not, but what was certain was that it was drinking beautifully. I was surprised how silky and refined it came across for a Pommard though…

posts.name: Paul Jaouen
posts.cooked: The ones I’ve opened at the 1er cru and lower have for the most part been drinking fine with maybe a couple needing more time. The couple of grand crus I’ve had were no where near ready.

posts.name: Mark Golodetz
posts.cooked: It is not homogenous-the best are seriously long term.

posts.name: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)
posts.cooked:
I cannot imagine that any of the better red Burgs 2008 are near full maturity, just the opossite!
I was served a 2008 Morey Village 6 weeks ago, and it was quite monolythic and reserved … still quite structured, drinkable but not a real pleasure in my personal view.
Sure - there might be wines that do not age well … in all vintages … but I try to avoid them for my cellar …
and I rather let my good 2008 sleeping …

There might also be bottles that have not been shipped/stored properly …

There might also be tasters who think that if a wine doesn´t taste like a nail board … it is fully mature …
posts.display_username: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)

posts.name: Matt Fleming
posts.cooked: If we use Mugnier’s Chambolle-Musigny as a guide, not all 2008s should be consumed young.

posts.name: Jeremy Bartlett
posts.cooked:
Thanks guys, that likely longevity on some of the premier crus seems to accord with my recollections on (from memory) Bertheau, Clavellier, Comte Liger Belair. But none of those were in the past year.

Interestingly I recall now being told on several occasions on Beaune visits a few years ago that 2009 would also not be for long ageing.

Cracked this tonight:

2008 Jean Louis Trapet Chambertin

Singing. There is a floral, stemmy lift to the dark fruit notes with some honest Gevrey earth in the background. Deeply fruited, sappy with fine grained texture and lovely acid on the elegant, silky length. Still young but by no means a stern or unyielding nail board (thanks Gerhard). That fine long finish speaks of plenty of future development.

posts.name: Bill Hansford
posts.cooked:
Ugh.

I read some talk about 2008’s aging early and then saw this 2008 Hubert Lignier Clos de la Roche note on cellartracker:

1/26/2016 - ************* WROTE: 94 Points
Medium brownish garnet. Very evolved color for the vintage as already noted by previous writers. Aromas of dark fruit, spices, tobacco and tertiary notes in general. On the palate this is soft and evolved, with good balance and high drinkability. A nice, mature GC Burg, but not in the same league as the 2002.
Given the evolved status of the aromas I would not wait for much longer.

So I decided to open one up a couple of months ago when out with wife at a restaurant.

Big mistake.

Turns out the above reviewed bottle must have been flawed in some fashion b/c it didn’t resemble what I had in any way whatsoever.

Nose was utterly primary and tight. Ended up being tight, young and structured as well. Honestly, I might end up waiting another 7-10 years before opening the next one.

Now that doesn’t mean that all 2008’s I’ve had are destined to last as long. 2008 Fourrier Gevrey Chambertin VV is showing a lot of acid but his 1er Cru bottlings - while still a bit acidic - are just beautiful, balanced and high fruit examples of what the vintage had to offer.

It is important to note though that I am (and have been since release) a huge fan of 2008 and frankly consider it one of my favorite vintages. So I am biased!

posts.name: George Chadwick
posts.cooked: The ones I’ve had are lean and high toned to the extent that I hope they get better because so far I don’t care for the vintage. No hint of premature aging though, in fact the acidity makes them seem bulletproof. A 2008 Clos des Reas open for three days at room temp last week showed no oxidation at all. Still too young.

posts.name: john stimson
posts.cooked: Which vintage since 1990 (perhaps aside from 2004) has fallen apart and is now over the hill? Usually when you hear this, it means the wines are transitioning from primary to secondary/tertiary characteristics and are in a dull phase. Winemakers all over the world prefer their wines much younger than the rest of us, and are often not a great source interms of when their wines should be enjoyed. I haven’t read Steen’s comments, but there’s no way in the world anyone should be worried about 2008 at 8 years of age.

posts.name: Howard Cooper
posts.cooked:
Jeremy Bartlett:

I see that Steen Ohman has passed on a general message from several vignerons that the 2008 reds are not for long term aging, and that some of his own imbibing bears that out with a noticably rapid aging over the past year (not falling apart he hastens to add).

Have others noticed this?

I had always pegged the 2008 reds for a longish sleep but will now sample a few.

Yes. I have had a few that seem mature all of a sudden. One example is Dublere Charmes Chambertin.

posts.name: Ryan Caughey
posts.cooked: Interesting thread. I had no plans to touch '08s any time soon. I’ll put one or two in the queue and take a look.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked: An 08 Bize Marconnets was surprisingly expressive the other day; Chisa Bize had said it was nice to drink now, and she was right. If not mature, then at least an adolescent, not a youngster.

posts.name: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)
posts.cooked:
Jeremy Bartlett:

…
Interestingly I recall now being told on several occasions on Beaune visits a few years ago that 2009 would also not be for long ageing.
…

Totally ridiculous statement … if we are talking about sound bottles and good producers!
2009 will outlive everything between 2005 and 2012 - except 2010.

john stimson:

Which vintage since 1990 (perhaps aside from 2004) has fallen apart and is now over the hill? …

None!
Most 1997, 1994, 1992, 1991 are not going to improve further, but that absolutely doesn´t mean that the better wines are “over the hill”.

1994 is the first canditate to slowly dry out over the next years, some Village and smaller 1ers were maybe better a few years ago … but usually, with enough air by slow oxing, most are still ok/good/vg now (because they were never really great).
There are also some “thin” 1992s and 1991s … (but those were always “thin”).
posts.display_username: Gerhard P.  (P r @ € s e n t)

posts.name: Robert Pavlovich
posts.cooked:
Good to know as I have the Lignier CdlR.

I opened a 2008 Bachelet Corbeaux and that definitely seemed like it would go a long distance. Now is far too soon.

Usually when you hear this, it means the wines are transitioning from primary to secondary/tertiary characteristics and are in a dull phase. Winemakers all over the world prefer their wines much younger than the rest of us, and are often not a great source interms of when their wines should be enjoyed. I haven’t read Steen’s comments, but there’s no way in the world anyone should be worried about 2008 at 8 years of age.

Very wise statement.

When to open your preferred bottles of wine for your enjoyment is purely your personal choice…

That being said…I enjoy reading everyone’s opinions !

posts.name: Jeremy Holmes
posts.cooked: Have had the '08 Rousseau Gevrey, '08 Thibault Liger-Belair Clos Vougeot and '08 Mugnier NSG ‘Fourches’ over the past couple of weeks. All three wide open and utterly delicious. All three will live for several decades more.

posts.name: yaacovbarselah
posts.cooked: jadot ursules?

posts.name: Jeremy Bartlett
posts.cooked:
I think what surprised me about the comments Steen passed on was that I had in keeping with some of the received wisdom categorised 2008 in with 1996 (lean, high acid, favouring the traditionally warmer sites in the CdN) and the village and 1er cru wines I tasted post-vintage were lean with bright high toned fruit and that acid. So I ‘lazily’ anticipated 1996 style development. A set of Drouhins a few years ago (2008 Griotte, Petits Monts and Amoureuses all in sensational form) didn’t quite fit the categorisation but I assumed they were outliers.

So if some wines are now opening up - perhaps there is less homogeneity than 1996 (if that is still the best comparison). I don’t think there should be any suggestion of them getting over the hill given the quality of fruit in the better wines and that acid spine.

Incidentally, re the comments on the 2009 vintage - I couldn’t fathom this and at the time put them down to a reaction to the non-classic qualities of the vintage. Those wines will be very long-lived.

Another data point: a 2008 Dujac CDR earlier this year was primary and ungiving but packed with potential.
